title: Backends: SB vs CRP
text: DPmixGPD supports two Bayesian nonparametric backends: SB (stick-breaking): explicit mixture weights CRP (Chinese Restaurant Process): allocation-based representation share kernel registry prediction interface.

title: Prediction
code: library(DPmixGPD) library(nimble) use_cached_fit <- TRUE .fit_path <- function(name) {   path <- system.file("extdata", name, package = "DPmixGPD")   if (path == "") path <- file.path("inst", "extdata", name)   path } fit_small <- readRDS(.fit_path("fit_small.rds")) set.seed(1) N <- 50 y <- abs(rnorm(N)) + 0.1  bundle <- build_nimble_bundle(   y = y,   backend = "sb",   kernel = "normal",   GPD = TRUE,   J = 8,   mcmc = list(niter = 200, nburnin = 50, thin = 1, nchains = 1, seed = 1) ) if (use_cached_fit) {   fit <- fit_small } else {   fit <- run_mcmc_bundle_manual(bundle, show_progress = FALSE) }

title: Prediction
code: y_grid <- seq(min(y), max(y), length.out = 60)  pr_den <- predict(fit, type = "density", y = y_grid) pr_surv <- predict(fit, type = "survival", y = y_grid)  plot(y_grid, pr_den$fit[1, ], type = "l", col = "steelblue",      xlab = "y", ylab = "density", main = "Predictive density") plot(y_grid, pr_surv$fit[1, ], type = "l", col = "firebrick",      xlab = "y", ylab = "survival", main = "Predictive survival")

title: Start Here: What DPmixGPD Does
text: Standard single-distribution fits throw hands data sparse tail: bulk dominates, heavy excursions ignored, probabilistic forecasts understate rare events. DPmixGPD targets precisely regime pairing flexible Dirichlet process mixture bulk dedicated generalized Pareto tail beyond threshold can keep inference honest extremes.

title: Start Here: What DPmixGPD Does
text: Bulk model: DP mixture kernels (normal, gamma, lognormal, Laplace, inverse Gaussian, Amoroso) delivers adaptively shaped density/conditional models. Tail model: Generalized Pareto Distribution glued learned threshold, smooth transitions optional covariate links. Prediction: fitted object can forecast densities, survival curves, quantiles, posterior predictive draws.

title: Start Here: What DPmixGPD Does
text: DPmixGPD concept: flexible bulk + GPD tail stitched threshold.
code: bulk <- data.frame(x = seq(0, 10, length.out = 200)) bulk$y <- dlnorm(bulk$x, meanlog = 0.5, sdlog = 0.4) * 0.8 tail <- data.frame(x = seq(8, 20, length.out = 3)) tail$y <- vapply(   tail$x,   DPmixGPD::dGpd,   numeric(1),   threshold = 10,   scale = 2,   shape = 0.3 ) ggplot() +   geom_area(data = bulk, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "#66c2a5", alpha = 0.7) +   geom_line(data = tail, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "#fc8d62", size = 1.2) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 10, linetype = "dashed") +   annotate("text", x = 12, y = 0.15, label = "GPD tail", color = "#fc8d62") +   annotate("text", x = 4, y = 0.25, label = "DP mixture bulk", color = "#66c2a5") +   labs(x = "Outcome", y = "Density")

title: Single-Outcome Modeling: Bulk + Tail in One Model
text: DPmixGPD targets positive, right-skewed outcomes. kernels (lognormal, gamma, inverse Gaussian, Amoroso, Laplace) assume support Y>0Y > 0. data contain negatives, monotonic transformation (e.g., shift + log) simplest path forward. Censoring (yet) unified, treat survival-specific problems via survival track.

title: Single-Outcome Modeling: Bulk + Tail in One Model
text: DP mixture bulk: Dirichlet process prior kernel parameters; mixture index jj controls components (equivalently truncation parameter JJ). Threshold u(x)u(x): constant covariate-linked; u(x)u(x) pivot chaining tail. GPD tail: estimates scale/shape exceedances Y>u(x)Y > u(x) coexists bulk predictions smooth. Posterior outputs: weights w[j]w[j], component means, thresholds, tail shape/scale.

code: dense_grid <- seq(0, max(y) + 5, length.out = 120) bulk_pred <- predict(fit, type = "density", y = dense_grid) bulk_df <- data.frame(y = dense_grid, density = bulk_pred$fit[1, ]) tail_df <- data.frame(y = dense_grid, density = 1 - bulk_df$density / max(bulk_df$density)) ggplot(bulk_df, aes(x = y, y = density)) +   geom_line(color = "#1f77b4") +   geom_line(data = tail_df, aes(y = density), color = "#ff7f0e", linetype = "dashed") +   labs(title = "Bulk vs Tail (scaled)", y = "Density estimate") tail_tab <- summary(fit)$table keep <- tail_tab$parameter %in% c("threshold[1]", "tail_shape") posterior_table <- tail_tab[keep, c("parameter", "mean", "sd", "q0.500")] posterior_table <- rbind(posterior_table, data.frame(   parameter = "predictive mean",   mean = mean(predict(fit, type = "mean", nsim_mean = 100)$fit),   sd = NA,   q0.500 = NA )) knitr::kable(posterior_table, digits = 3)

title: Backends: CRP vs Stick-Breaking
text: SB rewrites mixture stick-breaking variables v_j clusters deterministic weights. CRP draws latent labels z_i lets Chinese Restaurant Process allocate points j components. use kernel, threshold, tail flags (GPD = TRUE/FALSE), estimated mixture weights stay aligned prediction.

title: Backends: CRP vs Stick-Breaking
text: Kernel families, tail configuration, predict.mixgpd_fit() logic. output mixgpd_fit object contains fields even one backend uses cluster labels uses sticks. Diagnostics (trace plots, tail index, q/j grid) can computed identically either backend.

title: Backends: CRP vs Stick-Breaking
text: CRP draws latent labels directly tends show slightly higher variance weights, SB trades variance deterministic mixture stick lengths. prediction stage honors whichever weights estimation produced.
code: y <- sim_bulk_tail(n = 150, tail_prob = 0.08, seed = 101) common_args <- list(   y = y,   kernel = "lognormal",   GPD = TRUE,   J = 6,   mcmc = list(niter = 200, nburnin = 50, thin = 2, nchains = 2, seed = c(1, 2)) )  if (use_cached_fit) {   fit_sb <- fit_small   fit_crp <- fit_small_crp   sb_time <- c(elapsed = NA_real_)   crp_time <- c(elapsed = NA_real_) } else {   sb_time <- system.time({     bundle_sb <- do.call(build_nimble_bundle, c(list(backend = "sb"), common_args))     fit_sb <- run_mcmc_bundle_manual(bundle_sb)   })   crp_time <- system.time({     bundle_crp <- do.call(build_nimble_bundle, c(list(backend = "crp"), common_args))     fit_crp <- run_mcmc_bundle_manual(bundle_crp)   }) }  system_time <- data.frame(   backend = c("SB", "CRP"),   elapsed = c(sb_time[["elapsed"]], crp_time[["elapsed"]]) ) system_time #>   backend elapsed #> 1      SB      NA #> 2     CRP      NA

title: Kernels: What They Mean and When to Use Which
text: DPmixGPD exposes registry kernel family provides density, quantile, RNG functions consistent names (dGammaMix, qGammaMix, rGammaMix, etc.). j-th component always shares parameter layout across kernels (location, scale, shape), components count controlled J .

title: Kernels: What They Mean and When to Use Which
text: data strictly positive? yes, stay (0, Inf) kernels (lognormal, gamma, amoroso, inverse Gaussian). need flexible tail shape within kernel (GPD kick-)? Choose Amoroso Cauchy wide tail control. Need interpretable mean/variance symmetric central mass? Use normal Laplace rely GPD extremes. dataset shows multiple modes, increase J keep kernel simple avoid identifiability issues.

title: Causal Inference with DPmixGPD: CQTE End-to-End
text: Requires: y outcome. T binary treatment indicator. X covariate matrix/data frame (columns x1, x2, x3 examples).

title: Causal Inference with DPmixGPD: CQTE End-to-End
text: Propensity score stage (T | X). Outcome model Y | X, T=0 Y | X, T=1, DP mixture + optional GPD. Predictions per arm new covariate grids. CQTE = quantile(trt) - quantile(con) probability covariate slice.

title: Causal Inference with DPmixGPD: CQTE End-to-End
text: CQTE surface tau=0.95 varying x1 / x2 x3=0.
code: grid <- expand.grid(   x1 = seq(-1.2, 1.2, length.out = 15),   x2 = seq(-0.8, 0.8, length.out = 5),   x3 = 0 ) taus <- c(.1, .5, .9, .95, .99) pr_trt <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$trt, newdata = grid, type = "quantile", p = taus) pr_con <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$con, newdata = grid, type = "quantile", p = taus) cqte_df <- data.frame(   x1 = rep(grid$x1, each = length(taus)),   tau = rep(taus, times = nrow(grid)),   cqte = c(pr_trt$fit - pr_con$fit) ) slice_df <- cqte_df[cqte_df$tau %in% c(.5, .95, .99) & cqte_df$x1 %in% c(-1, 0, 1), ] ggplot(slice_df, aes(x = x1, y = cqte, color = factor(tau))) +   geom_line() +   labs(title = "CQTE slices (selected taus)", color = "tau") surf_grid <- expand.grid(x1 = seq(-1, 1, length.out = 25), x2 = seq(-1, 1, length.out = 25), x3 = 0) tau_surface <- 0.95 surf_trt <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$trt, newdata = surf_grid, type = "quantile", p = tau_surface) surf_con <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$con, newdata = surf_grid, type = "quantile", p = tau_surface) surf_grid$cqte <- as.numeric(surf_trt$fit - surf_con$fit) ggplot(surf_grid, aes(x = x1, y = x2, fill = cqte)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C") +   labs(title = "CQTE surface (tau = 0.95)", fill = "CQTE") tail_cqte <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$trt, newdata = data.frame(x1 = 0, x2 = 0, x3 = 0), type = "quantile", p = c(.95, .99, .995)) control_tail <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$con, newdata = data.frame(x1 = 0, x2 = 0, x3 = 0), type = "quantile", p = c(.95, .99, .995)) data.frame(   tau = c(.95, .99, .995),   cqte = tail_cqte$fit - control_tail$fit ) #>     tau     cqte.1    cqte.2   cqte.3 #> 1 0.950 -0.9250932 -2.533016 -3.05655 #> 2 0.990 -0.9250932 -2.533016 -3.05655 #> 3 0.995 -0.9250932 -2.533016 -3.05655

code: summary_points <- data.frame(   x1 = c(-1, 0, 1),   x2 = c(0, 0, 0),   label = c("x1 = -1, x2 = 0", "x1 = 0, x2 = 0", "x1 = 1, x2 = 0"),   cqte_median = NA_real_,   cqte_tail = NA_real_ ) for (i in seq_len(nrow(summary_points))) {   newdata <- data.frame(x1 = summary_points$x1[i], x2 = summary_points$x2[i], x3 = 0)   med <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$trt, newdata = newdata, type = "quantile", p = 0.5)$fit   med_con <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$con, newdata = newdata, type = "quantile", p = 0.5)$fit   tail <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$trt, newdata = newdata, type = "quantile", p = 0.95)$fit   tail_con <- predict(cf$outcome_fit$con, newdata = newdata, type = "quantile", p = 0.95)$fit   summary_points$cqte_median[i] <- med - med_con   summary_points$cqte_tail[i] <- tail - tail_con } knitr::kable(summary_points[, c("label", "cqte_median", "cqte_tail")], digits = 3)

text: CQTE subtracts conditional quantiles levels tau treated (trt) vs control (con) arms covariate point. ATE averages mean outcomes; CATE averages conditional means. CQTE instead tracks tail behavior matter extremes. CQTE flat across tau, treatment effect depend extremeness; rising curves point tail dominance.

DPmixGPD currently treats outcomes positive durations uses conventional density + tail augmentation, censoring handled via manual likelihood reweighting outside package (future work may integrate censored nodes inside Nimble).

title: Survival Outcomes: Censoring + Heavy Tails
text: treat quantiles inverse survival: higher quantile, lower survival probability, mirrors extreme durations dominate upper tails. ## Roadmap assumptions Overlap assumption: model must see enough events (status = 1) learn tail parameters; make GPD estimate unstable. Censoring note: handle censoring outside DPmixGPD imputing truncated observations reweighting data bundling. title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors
text: note NIMBLE normal complex models; flags variables touched yet. Always run fit$model$initializeInfo() confirm truly missing nodes. short example logs message shows inspect: warning persists, check bundle$dimensions explicit initial values bundle$inits ensure every latent vector compatible length.
code: bundle <- build_nimble_bundle(y = abs(rnorm(40)) + 0.1, backend = "sb", kernel = "normal", J = 5) if (use_cached_fit) {   fit <- fit_small } else {   fit <- run_mcmc_bundle_manual(bundle, show_progress = FALSE) } fit$bundle$code  # ensures model objects exist #> NULL

title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors
text: Mixing data different numbers covariates quickly raises dimension errors. Always confirm ncol(newdata) == ncol(bundle$design$data$X) calling predict().
code: bad_X <- matrix(1, nrow = 5, ncol = 1) stopifnot(ncol(bad_X) == ncol(bundle$dimensions$X %||% matrix()))

title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors
text: class errors arises components truncation small epsilon drives many weight draws zero. Remedy: increase components (JJ) raise epsilon slightly. following histogram shows posterior weight spread: Posterior weights survived truncation.
code: ws <- draw_w <- DPmixGPD:::.extract_draws_matrix(fit, drop_v = TRUE) weight_cols <- grep("^w\\[[0-9]+\\]$", colnames(draw_w), value = TRUE) weight_df <- data.frame(weight = c(draw_w[, weight_cols])) ggplot(weight_df, aes(x = weight)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "#1f77b4", color = "white") +   labs(title = "Posterior weight distribution (some components may collapse)")

title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors
text: trace plots show drift, lengthen nburnin, increase thin, reduce niter raw CPU pressure. Try plot(fit) diagnostics thresholds tail shape, use plot(fit, family = "traceplot", params = "alpha") monitor concentration.

title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors
text: tail shape/scale oscillate, usual remedies : Prior sensitivity: tighten tail_shape priors (e.g., param_specs = list(tail_shape = list(mode = "fixed", value = 0.2))). exceedances: raise threshold setting higher initial guess increasing J. GPD flag: temporarily set GPD = FALSE ensure bulk stable reintroducing tail.

title: Troubleshooting and Common Errors

title: Developer Guide: Adding a Kernel / Extending the Engine
text: Call init_kernel_registry() inspect current labels slot names. entry needs density, quantile, rng, nimble stubs following naming pattern d<Name>Mix, q<Name>Mix, r<Name>Mix, d<Name>. mixture index j always component number. support: e.g., (0, Inf) (-Inf, Inf), params: names location/scale/shape, nimble: generated Nimble code snippet kernel density.

title: Developer Guide: Adding a Kernel / Extending the Engine
text: Register kernel using init_kernel_registry() lazy-loaded metadata; backend generator picks names automatically get_kernel_registry().
code: # Dummy skeleton (copy into R/0-base-kernels.R and register) nimbleFunction(   run = function(y = double(1), mu = double(0), sigma = double(0)) {     returnType(double(0))     dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = 1)   } )

title: DPmixGPD: AI Coding Agent Instructions
text: R package (R >= 4.5.0) Bayesian nonparametric Dirichlet Process mixture models flexible kernels optional GPD tail augmentation. Uses NIMBLE MCMC compilation two backends: stick-breaking (SB) Chinese Restaurant Process (CRP).

title: DPmixGPD: AI Coding Agent Instructions
code: # Phase 1: Specification (validates inputs, NO NIMBLE code generation) spec <- compile_model_spec(y, kernel = "gamma", backend = "crp", GPD = TRUE)  # Phase 2: Bundle (generates NIMBLE code, compiles sampler) bundle <- build_nimble_bundle(spec)  # Can also call directly with y, X, kernel, etc.  # Phase 3: MCMC (runs posterior sampling → mixgpd_fit object) fit <- run_mcmc_bundle_manual(bundle, niter = 1000, nburnin = 100)

title: DPmixGPD: AI Coding Agent Instructions
text: kernel properties stored registry initialized .onLoad() (see R/00-kernel-registry.R) Available kernels: gamma, lognormal, normal, laplace, invgauss, amoroso, cauchy Query registry kernel operation: kreg <- get_kernel_registry(); kernel_def <- kreg[[kernel_name]] kernel defines: bulk_params, bulk_support, allow_gpd, backend-specific density functions

title: DPmixGPD: AI Coding Agent Instructions
text: Pattern: {d|p|q|r} + Kernel + [Mix] + [Gpd] - dGamma() — base kernel density - dGammaMix() — mixture density (SB backend) - dGammaMixGpd() — mixture GPD tail - 4 variants (d/p/q/r) auto-generated kernel

title: DPmixGPD: AI Coding Agent Instructions
text: mixgpd_fit: Fitted model posterior samples (run_mcmc_bundle_manual()) dpmixgpd_bundle: Full MCMC bundle execution (build_nimble_bundle()) Standard methods: summary(), plot(), coef(), fitted(), predict(), residuals(), logLik(), vcov() Plotting via ggplot2 + ggmcmc::ggs() trace/density plots

what: Amoroso mixture with a GPD tail — amoroso_mixgpd
title: Amoroso mixture with a GPD tail — amoroso_mixgpd
text: family splices generalized Pareto distribution (GPD) threshold threshold onto Amoroso mixture bulk. Let \(F_{mix}\) denote Amoroso mixture CDF. spliced CDF \(F(x)=F_{mix}(x)\) \(x<threshold\) \(F(x)=F_{mix}(threshold) + \left\{1-F_{mix}(threshold)\right\}G(x)\) \(x\ge threshold\), \(G\) GPD CDF exceedances threshold.
